steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what "conceivable" most nearly means as used in the passage. Mary Seacole writes that nature threw "every conceivable obstacle" in the way — the word describes the full range of obstacles that could be imagined or thought of.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Possible': Matches — "conceivable" comes from conceive (to think of or imagine), so it means something that can be imagined or is possible. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Regular': "Regular obstacles" would mean ordinary or routine ones, not every obstacle one could imagine. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Visible': Relates to sight, not to the scope of imagination that "conceivable" conveys. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Basic': "Basic obstacles" implies simple or fundamental ones, not the full imaginable range. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
